problems.title: Hello, Gopher
problems.statement: Return the exact greeting string for a supplied name.
problems.explanation: This problem practices strings, basics in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(name string) string { return "Hello, "+name+"!" }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("Ada"), "Hello, Ada!"); assertEqual(Solve("Go"), "Hello, Go!") }
problems.lesson:
Go rewards direct programs: declare data, loop over it, print or return a result. In early Go practice, focus less on clever abstractions and more on knowing exactly where values flow. A Go program starts at package main and func main, but problem solving usually means writing one small function that can be tested repeatedly.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: Read the input shape, choose the plainest type, write a loop, and handle the empty case first. If you can explain every line aloud, you are writing Go correctly.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Hello, Gopher by choosing the simplest representation for strings, basics. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

problems.title: Temperature Table
problems.statement: Convert a list of Fahrenheit temperatures to Celsius, rounded toward zero.
problems.explanation: This problem practices loops, arithmetic in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(f []int) []int { out:=make([]int,len(f)); for i,x:=range f { out[i]=(x-32)*5/9 }; return out }
problems.lesson:

problems.approach: Approach: solve Temperature Table by choosing the simplest representation for loops, arithmetic. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

problems.title: Unique Lines
problems.statement: Given lines of text, return the first occurrence of each line preserving order.
problems.explanation: This problem practices maps, slices in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(lines []string) []string { seen:=map[string]bool{}; out:=[]string{}; for _,s:=range lines { if !seen[s] {seen[s]=true; out=append(out,s)} }; return out }
problems.lesson:

A Go program starts at package main and func main, but problem solving usually means writing one small function that can be tested repeatedly.

problems.approach: Approach: solve Unique Lines by choosing the simplest representation for maps, slices. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.

problems.title: Byte Popcount
problems.statement: Count the number of set bits in an unsigned 8-bit integer.
problems.explanation: This problem practices bits, functions in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(x uint8) int { c:=0; for x>0 { c+=int(x&1); x >>= 1 }; return c }
problems.lesson:
Go programs are built from declarations. A name exists only in its scope, and short declarations with := are convenient but can accidentally create a new local variable instead of updating an outer one. Learning scope early prevents many confusing bugs.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: Prefer explicit variable names. Use := for first declarations and = for updates. Keep variables close to where they are used.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Byte Popcount by choosing the simplest representation for bits, functions. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

problems.title: Shadow Sum
problems.statement: Simulate block-scoped updates: for each number add it to sum; if it is even, add it again as a temporary bonus.
problems.explanation: This problem practices control-flow in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(nums []int) int { sum:=0; for _,x:=range nums { sum+=x; if x%2==0 { y:=x; sum+=y } }; return sum }
problems.lesson:
Go programs are built from declarations. A name exists only in its scope, and short declarations with := are convenient but can accidentally create a new local variable instead of updating an outer one. Learning scope early prevents many confusing bugs.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: Prefer explicit variable names. Use := for first declarations and = for updates. Keep variables close to where they are used.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Shadow Sum by choosing the simplest representation for control-flow. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

problems.title: Comma Format
problems.statement: Format a non-negative integer string with commas every three digits.
problems.explanation: This problem practices strings in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(s string) string { n:=len(s); if n<=3 {return s}; r:=n%3; out:=""; if r>0 {out=s[:r]; if r<n {out+=","}}; for i:=r;i<n;i+=3 { out+=s[i:i+3]; if i+3<n {out+=","} }; return out }
problems.lesson:
Strings in Go are immutable byte sequences, usually UTF-8 encoded. Indexing a string gives bytes, while ranging over a string gives runes. Numeric operations are explicit and integer division truncates toward zero.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: Ask: am I processing bytes, ASCII characters, or Unicode runes? That single question prevents most string bugs.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Comma Format by choosing the simplest representation for strings. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

problems.title: Unicode Anagram
problems.statement: Return true if two strings contain the same runes with the same multiplicities.
problems.explanation: This problem practices unicode, maps in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(a,b string) bool { m:=map[rune]int{}; for _,r:=range a {m[r]++}; for _,r:=range b {m[r]--}; for _,v:=range m { if v!=0 {return false} }; return true }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("silent","listen"), true); assertEqual(Solve("go","goo"), false); assertEqual(Solve("夜猫","猫夜"), true) }
problems.lesson:
Strings in Go are immutable byte sequences, usually UTF-8 encoded. Indexing a string gives bytes, while ranging over a string gives runes. Numeric operations are explicit and integer division truncates toward zero.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: Ask: am I processing bytes, ASCII characters, or Unicode runes? That single question prevents most string bugs.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Unicode Anagram by choosing the simplest representation for unicode, maps. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

problems.title: Rotate Slice
problems.statement: Rotate a slice left by k positions and return a new slice.
problems.explanation: This problem practices slices in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(nums []int,k int) []int { n:=len(nums); if n==0 {return []int{}}; k%=n; out:=append([]int{}, nums[k:]...); out=append(out, nums[:k]...); return out }
problems.lessonTitle: Slices, maps, structs, and JSON-shaped data
problems.lesson:
Composite types let you model real data. Slices are views over arrays, maps are hash tables with useful zero values, and structs give names to fields. Most Go interview-style problems become simple once you pick the right composite type.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: Use slices for ordered sequences, maps for membership/counting, and structs when fields have meaning.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Rotate Slice by choosing the simplest representation for slices. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

problems.title: Word Frequency
problems.statement: Return the count for each word after lowercasing ASCII letters.
problems.explanation: This problem practices maps, strings in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.

problems.title: Nested List Depth
problems.statement: Given a JSON-like nested integer list encoded as interface values, compute max nesting depth.
problems.explanation: This problem practices recursion in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(v any) int { switch x:=v.(type){ case []any: best:=1; for _,e:=range x { if d:=1+Solve(e); d>best {best=d} }; return best; default: return 0 } }
problems.lesson:
Functions in Go are values with clear parameter and return types. Good Go functions are small, named around what they compute, and easy to test. Recursion is useful for tree-shaped or nested data but should have an obvious base case.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: Write the base case before the recursive case. Return values instead of mutating global state.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Nested List Depth by choosing the simplest representation for recursion. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

problems.title: Deferred Product
problems.statement: Process numbers left-to-right, but multiply positive numbers as if deferred in reverse; return the same final product.
problems.explanation: This problem practices defer, loops in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(nums []int) int { p:=1; for _,x:=range nums { if x>0 {p*=x} }; return p }
problems.lesson:
Functions in Go are values with clear parameter and return types. Good Go functions are small, named around what they compute, and easy to test. Recursion is useful for tree-shaped or nested data but should have an obvious base case.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: Write the base case before the recursive case. Return values instead of mutating global state.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Deferred Product by choosing the simplest representation for defer, loops. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

problems.title: Point Manhattan
problems.statement: Implement a method-style helper concept: return Manhattan distance between two points.
problems.explanation: This problem practices structs, methods in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.lesson:
A method is a function with a receiver. Methods let a type own behavior without hiding data behind a large class hierarchy. Pointer receivers mutate or avoid copying; value receivers are good for small immutable values.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: If the method changes the receiver, use a pointer receiver. Otherwise keep it value-based unless the type is large.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Point Manhattan by choosing the simplest representation for structs, methods. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.

pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.

problems.title: Count Bytes
problems.statement: Given chunks from an abstract reader, count total bytes.
problems.explanation: This problem practices interfaces, io in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(chunks []string) int { n:=0; for _,s:=range chunks {n+=len([]byte(s))}; return n }
problems.lesson:
Interfaces describe behavior, not data. In Go, a type satisfies an interface implicitly by having the required methods. This makes small interfaces powerful: io.Reader, sort.Interface, fmt.Stringer.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: Accept interfaces when you need behavior. Return concrete types when you create values.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Count Bytes by choosing the simplest representation for interfaces, io. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

hints: Start with the simplest data representation.

problems.title: Pipeline Sum
problems.statement: Use goroutine-style thinking: return the sum of squares of all numbers.
problems.explanation: This problem practices concurrency, channels in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(nums []int) int { s:=0; for _,x:=range nums {s+=x*x}; return s }
problems.lesson:
Concurrency in Go is about composing independent activities. Goroutines run functions concurrently; channels coordinate communication. Even when solving sequential exercises, pipeline thinking helps separate stages: produce, transform, collect.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: Do not start goroutines just to look fancy. First design the data flow; then add concurrency only if it makes the flow clearer or faster.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Pipeline Sum by choosing the simplest representation for concurrency, channels. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.

problems.title: Merge Sorted Streams
problems.statement: Merge two sorted integer streams into one sorted slice.
problems.explanation: This problem practices concurrency, merge in the spirit of the Go book chapter. Focus on small, idiomatic Go: clear types, simple loops, and explicit error-free control flow. The intended solution avoids cleverness and shows how Go programs compose primitives into readable code.
hints: Start with the simplest data representation.
Prefer explicit loops and small helper functions.
Think about zero values and empty inputs.
problems.solutionCode:
package main
func Solve(a,b []int) []int { i,j:=0,0; out:=[]int{}; for i<len(a)||j<len(b){ if j==len(b)||(i<len(a)&&a[i]<=b[j]){out=append(out,a[i]); i++}else{out=append(out,b[j]); j++} }; return out }
problems.lesson:
Concurrency in Go is about composing independent activities. Goroutines run functions concurrently; channels coordinate communication. Even when solving sequential exercises, pipeline thinking helps separate stages: produce, transform, collect.

Study goal: understand the idea before touching the editor. For this problem, identify the input type, the output type, and the one transformation between them. Then write a tiny Go function that expresses that transformation directly.

Key Go habit: Do not start goroutines just to look fancy. First design the data flow; then add concurrency only if it makes the flow clearer or faster.
problems.approach: Approach: solve Merge Sorted Streams by choosing the simplest representation for concurrency, merge. Start with the zero/empty input. Then add the main loop or helper. Keep the function deterministic: same input, same output, no hidden global state.
pitfalls: Forgetting Go zero values can either help or hurt depending on the type.
Overengineering with extra structs/interfaces before the simple function works.
Not testing empty, one-element, and repeated-value cases.
